other_observers: Andrew Spencer; Bill Schmoker and others on the Bonny CBC
specific_location: 8 birds were at the DOW bunkhouse below the Bonny dam, while the other 6 or more were in the Hale State Wildlife Area along the road about 2 miles farther east
description: The birds were seen well on numerous occasions. The males were extremely distinctive, like House Finches but a little plumper, and with the bright raspberry-red stain continuous all over the head and down the breast, with darker auriculars and white belly and ventral area. Females were pretty distinctive also, also plump finches with very sharply defined facial patterns, unlike House Finch: a strong white supercilium and strong white moustachial stripe setting off the dark brown crown, auriculars, and submoustachial stripe. The females were also more crisply streaked below than House Finches, with white bellies and ventral areas like the males.Behaviors: The birds were flying around the area in small flocks and landing in the tops of tall trees. They would make their distinctive calls while perched, though the louder versions were made in flight.Call: Birds made the typical Purple Finch flight call, an extremely brief high-pitched "tip" or "tp" call that is distinctive and very different from the flight calls of Cassin's and House Finches. For what it's worth, the flight call of Purple Finch is not unlike the flight call of Lawrence's Goldfinch, though a little less musical.Plumage: male and female
